name: Xolotl
description: Xolotl, the Aztec god of lightning, fire, death, monsters, misfortune, sickness, and deformities, is often depicted as a dog-headed man or a skeleton. He is the twin brother of Quetzalcoatl, and together they represent duality and transformation. Xolotl played a crucial role in the creation myths, specifically in the Fifth Sun, where he helped Quetzalcoatl retrieve the bones of previous generations from the underworld, Mictlan, to recreate humanity. He is known for his self-sacrificing nature, as he initially resisted being sacrificed to set the sun in motion, transforming himself into various forms like an agave plant, a double corn stalk, and an axolotl to avoid his fate. This association with transformation and duality makes him a complex and intriguing figure in Aztec mythology. He is also considered the psychopomp, guiding the dead on their journey to Mictlan and protecting the sun as it travels through the underworld each night. Xolotl's canine form reflects his connection to the underworld and his role as a guardian, while his association with monsters and deformities highlights the darker aspects of the cosmos and the unpredictable nature of life. His name translates to 'animal', and he embodies the wild, untamed forces of nature. He is a god of transitions, both physical and spiritual, and a reminder of the cyclical nature of existence.
subtitle: God of lightning, dogs, and the underworld.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: Xolotl helps Quetzalcoatl retrieve the bones of previous humans from Mictlan to create the current generation, demonstrating his vital role in the Aztec creation myth.

keyMyths.title: Xolotl's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: To set the Fifth Sun in motion, the gods had to sacrifice themselves. Xolotl resisted, transforming into various forms before ultimately succumbing, showing his connection to transformation.
keyMyths.source: Historia de los Mexicanos por sus pinturas

keyMyths.title: Guiding the Dead
keyMyths.description: As a psychopomp, Xolotl guides the souls of the deceased through the perils of Mictlan, the underworld, ensuring their safe passage to the afterlife.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: Xolotl's worship often involved animal sacrifices, particularly dogs, which were seen as his earthly representatives. Human sacrifices were less common but not unheard of, especially during times of great need or to appease the god's darker aspects.

worshipPractices.name: Ritualistic Games
worshipPractices.description: The Mesoamerican ballgame, Ullamaliztli, was associated with Xolotl. The game symbolized the cosmic struggle between light and darkness, and the players' movements mirrored Xolotl's journey through the underworld. The losers of the game were sometimes sacrificed.

festivals.name: Toxcatl
festivals.timing: During the fifth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: While primarily dedicated to Tezcatlipoca, Toxcatl also acknowledged Xolotl's role as a god of transformation and change. Ritual dances and offerings were made to both deities during this festival.

festivals.name: Xocotl Huetzi
festivals.timing: During the tenth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: This festival, dedicated to the fire god Xiuhtecuhtli, also recognized Xolotl's association with fire and lightning. It involved the raising of a tall pole and a ritualistic competition to climb it, symbolizing the ascent of the sun.

cultural.modernLegacy: Xolotl's image persists in Mexican art, literature, and popular culture, often representing transformation, duality, and the hidden aspects of human nature. He remains a compelling figure in contemporary interpretations of Aztec mythology.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Xolotl's origins are intertwined with the myth of Quetzalcoatl, his twin brother. Born from the virgin goddess Coatlicue, they represent duality – Quetzalcoatl as the feathered serpent of light and wisdom, and Xolotl as the dog-headed god of darkness and transformation. Some accounts say Xolotl was born from a flint knife. His birth was an integral part of the Aztec creation narrative, setting the stage for his crucial role in the subsequent eras of the world. He is often associated with the evening star, Venus, as Quetzalcoatl is with the morning star, further emphasizing their complementary nature. He embodies the concept of the ‘nahual’, a shapeshifting spirit animal closely linked to a person, representing their hidden potential and darker aspects.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Xolotl's most significant role lies in the myth of the Fifth Sun. After the previous suns were destroyed, the gods needed to create a new one, but the bones of the previous humans were required. Xolotl accompanied Quetzalcoatl to Mictlan, the underworld, to retrieve these bones from Mictlantecuhtli, the god of death. Despite Mictlantecuhtli's attempts to thwart them, Xolotl and Quetzalcoatl succeeded, though Xolotl was instrumental in escaping, using his cunning and shapeshifting abilities. The bones were then ground and sprinkled with the gods' blood, creating the humans of the Fifth Sun. In another myth, Xolotl, fearing sacrifice, transformed himself into various forms – a young maize plant, a maguey plant with two stalks, and finally, an axolotl (a type of salamander) – before he was eventually captured and sacrificed. This story explains the origin of the axolotl and its connection to the god.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Xolotl was less widespread than that of other major Aztec deities like Quetzalcoatl or Huitzilopochtli, but it was still significant, particularly in communities that saw themselves as descendants of the earlier, destroyed worlds. His temples were often located on the outskirts of cities, reflecting his association with the margins and the underworld. Priests dedicated to Xolotl performed rituals to appease his darker aspects and to ensure safe passage through times of transition and change. Dogs, considered his earthly representatives, were sometimes sacrificed in his honor. The Mesoamerican ballgame, Ullamaliztli, was also heavily associated with Xolotl, serving as a symbolic reenactment of his journey through the underworld and his struggle against the forces of death.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Xolotl's legacy extends beyond his specific myths and rituals. He embodies the Aztec understanding of duality, transformation, and the acceptance of the darker aspects of existence. His role as a psychopomp, guiding souls to the afterlife, highlights the importance of death and rebirth in Aztec cosmology. In modern Mexico, Xolotl's image continues to appear in art, literature, and popular culture, often representing the hidden potential within individuals and the transformative power of facing one's fears. The axolotl, his animal form, has become a symbol of Mexico's unique biodiversity and a reminder of the interconnectedness of all living things. Xolotl's enduring presence reflects the continued fascination with Aztec mythology and its relevance to contemporary issues of identity, transformation, and the relationship between life and death.

shortDescription: Aztec god of lightning, death, and transformation. Twin of Quetzalcoatl, he guides souls and represents duality. Often depicted as a dog-headed man.
